Ralph Fiennes, John Lithgow, Stanley Tucci, Isabella Rossellini, Sergio Castellitto, Lucian Masmati, Carlos Diehz. The director is Edward Berger, who presided to the awesome "All quiet on the Western Front". The film follows the thriller novel of the same name, written by the renown author Robert Harris (writer of "Fatherland"). Scenery, costumes, and visuals are fantastic, although with a couple of annoying défaillances.

Surely, they have tried to make this film a masterpiece... Ralph Fiennes' facial expressions are themselves worthy of a prize and I am sure he plays well the role of the old and dithering Cardinal Thomas Lawrence (Jacopo Lomeli in the book). I read the novel and, up to 95%, I found it very good. The final is indeed quite controversial (and a bit off with the rest of the story), to say the least.
textOriginal:
Ralph Fiennes, John Lithgow, Stanley Tucci, Isabella Rossellini, Sergio Castellitto, Lucian Masmati, Carlos Diehz. The director is Edward Berger, who presided to the awesome "All quiet on the Western Front". The film follows the thriller novel of the same name, written by the renown author Robert Harris (writer of "Fatherland"). Scenery, costumes, and visuals are fantastic, although with a couple of annoying défaillances.

Surely, they have tried to make this film a masterpiece... Ralph Fiennes' facial expressions are themselves worthy of a prize and I am sure he plays well the role of the old and dithering Cardinal Thomas Lawrence (Jacopo Lomeli in the book). I read the novel and, up to 95%, I found it very good. The final is indeed quite controversial (and a bit off with the rest of the story), to say the least.

textDisplay: @maxdepasquale2351 a mockery of the Catholic Church is never entertainment, no matter how good the acting is. Fellow Catholics ought to be outraged at this garbage, not proposing it for an award.
textOriginal: @maxdepasquale2351 a mockery of the Catholic Church is never entertainment, no matter how good the acting is. Fellow Catholics ought to be outraged at this garbage, not proposing it for an award.

textDisplay:
@NicoleE12388 Indeed, that's one reason why I strongly despise the works by Dan Brown, no matter how successful the films and the selling of his "novels" have been. For me it's utter garbage.

However, "Conclave" is quite different, I think. I read the novel and there is little despising, mockery or disinformation about the Church, of her foundations and of her beliefs. On the contrary, the main character of the plot fights tooth and nails to prevent unworthy candidates to become the Pope, and he does so despite his dithering faith - which returns strong toward the end.

As for Benitez, I wrote time and again that personally I would have NOT allowed "her" to become a presbyter, let alone Pope. But I added that things - circumstances of life that protagonists found themselves in - are not black and white. Before issuing flat-out condemnations, do you know that the director also oversaw "All quiet on the Western front", that masterpiecely paints the horrors that young and less young human beings experience and go through in a real war?

Find my comments and read them, please.
textOriginal:
@NicoleE12388 Indeed, that's one reason why I strongly despise the works by Dan Brown, no matter how successful the films and the selling of his "novels" have been. For me it's utter garbage.
